items.title: Flow orchestrator — autonomous dev cycle after human approval
items.description:
The tribunal brain is state-of-the-art at deciding WHAT to work on. But once work starts, Flow goes blind. Every lane transition requires a human to pull. This item makes Flow active: a persistent orchestrator that understands the full development cycle from the board's lane config and drives work forward autonomously.

**The human contract:**
Humans do one thing: approve an item to start (`ready`). Everything after that is Flow's responsibility until the item is `done` or hits a blocker that genuinely needs a human decision.

**How it works:**
Flow runs as a persistent process watching `backlog.json` and `handoff_results/`. On any state change, it:
1. Reads the board's `config.statuses` — the lane labels are the development cycle
2. Reasons about what each lane means (coding, review, testing, deploy — whatever the team configured)
3. Identifies what needs to happen next for every in-flight item
4. Initiates the right agent for the current lane with full context
5. Processes the agent's result and advances the item to the next lane
6. On rejection/blocker: writes a thread, moves item back, re-initiates the responsible agent
7. On stale items: escalates with context

**Semantic lane understanding:**
Flow doesn't need explicit config like `qa watches code-review`. It reads the lane label and reasons from it — the same way a human engineer knows what 'code review' means without being told. This means it works for any workflow: `in-progress → QA → staging → done` is understood just as naturally as `in-progress → code-review → done`.

**Agent initiation:**
When an item enters a lane, Flow picks the best-fit agent from `config.agents` using the tribunal's agent_fit lens — skills, workload, history. It invokes them via `backlog handoff` (for work lanes) or `backlog review` (for review lanes), passing full context including what the previous agent did.

**Human re-engagement (only when necessary):**
- A blocker the agent cannot resolve
- An item reopened 3+ times (reopen_count threshold)
- A policy violation that requires a decision
- No agents available with the right skills

Everything else: Flow handles it.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `backlog orchestrate` starts the persistent process
- Human approves item to `ready` → orchestrator picks it up, assigns agent, invokes handoff — no further human action needed
- Agent completes → result processed → item advances to next lane → next agent initiated automatically
- Review agent rejects → thread written → item back to in-progress → original agent re-initiated with rejection context
- Item reaches terminal lane (`done`) → orchestrator logs completion, surfaces summary
- Works with any lane config — no hardcoded lane names
- Human is only re-engaged when genuinely blocked

links.reason: Handoff is the execution primitive the orchestrator uses to initiate agents
Result ingestion is how the orchestrator processes what agents produce and closes the loop

items.title: Enforce review gate regardless of lane config
items.description:
Removing a code-review lane from the board config should not silently remove the code review practice. Flow must treat peer review as a hard quality gate — not a lane feature.

**The rule:** Before any item reaches the terminal lane (`done`), a different agent than the one who built it must approve the work. This is enforced by the orchestrator, not by lane config.

**If no review lane exists in config.statuses:**
The orchestrator injects an inline review step automatically — picks the best-fit reviewer agent (different from assigned_to), invokes `backlog review`, waits for verdict. The item does not advance to done without a pass. No visible lane is created — this happens transparently in the orchestrator's execution.

**Config:**
```json
"orchestrator": {
  "require_review": true  // default
}
```
Setting `require_review: false` is allowed but Flow warns at startup:
> ⚠ require_review is disabled — items will reach done without peer review.

**Reviewer selection:**
Orchestrator picks the reviewer using the same agent_fit lens as the tribunal — highest skill match, lowest workload, has not worked on this item. If no suitable reviewer exists, item is flagged and human is notified.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Item built by backend-dev cannot reach done without a different agent approving
- Board with no code-review lane still enforces inline review
- `require_review: false` disables the gate with a loud warning
- Reviewer is never the same agent who implemented the item
- No suitable reviewer available → human notified, item paused

links.reason: Orchestrator is responsible for enforcing this gate at every lane transition

items.title: Auto mode vs Supervised mode
items.description:
Two fundamentally different ways to run Flow — not a feature toggle but a product mode that changes who is in control.

**Lead agent config:**
Lead is a role param on an existing agent entry — not a separate agent type:
```json
"config": {
  "agents": {
    "backend-dev": {
      "role": "lead",
      "skills": ["python", "api"]
    }
  }
}
```
Exactly one agent may have `"role": "lead"`. If zero or more than one are configured, the orchestrator hard-errors at startup with a clear message listing the conflict. No silent fallback.

**Supervised mode (default):**
Human controls when work starts. Human moves items to `ready` — orchestrator only acts on `ready` and beyond. Drives execution, manages agents, processes results, handles review. Human re-engages only on genuine blockers.

The human's role: decide what gets started. Not how it gets done.

**Auto mode:**
Lead agent continuously drives the full cycle:
1. Runs tribunal, picks highest-value item from backlog
2. Refines it — reads description, identifies gaps
3. When unsure or context is insufficient: asks human targeted questions (max 2 at a time, most blocking first). **Never guesses or builds something wrong for the sake of autonomy.**
4. Once refined, moves item to ready, orchestrator takes over
5. Picks next item, keeps going until user says stop

Human's role in auto mode: answer questions when the lead agent asks. That's it.

**Both modes — agent honesty rule:**
In any mode, agents must stop and ask the human rather than make assumptions when context is insufficient. Autonomy does not mean guessing. An agent that builds the wrong thing is worse than an agent that pauses.

**Config:**
```json
"orchestrator": {
  "mode": "supervised"  // or "auto"
}
```

**Stopping auto mode:**
`backlog orchestrate --stop` or Ctrl+C. In-progress items are not disrupted — they run to completion, no new items are picked up.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Exactly one `role: lead` agent required; zero or multiple → hard error at startup with agent names listed
- `supervised` mode: orchestrator ignores backlog/refined items, only acts on ready+
- `auto` mode: lead agent picks top item, refines with human Q&A when needed, moves to ready, loops
- Both modes: agent pauses and asks human when context is insufficient — never guesses
- Switching modes mid-flight does not disrupt in-progress items
- `--stop` halts auto loop after current in-flight items complete

links.reason: Mode determines how the orchestrator decides when to initiate agents and when to pause for human input
Both modes must enforce the review gate — auto mode does it transparently, supervised mode does it explicitly

items.title: Two-stage approval gate for external actions
items.description:
Before any irreversible side effect (posting to GitHub, deploying, sending messages, merging), Flow must stage the action and surface it for inspection. Nothing executes until an explicit /approve. Pattern: stage → inspect → approve/reject.

**Why**: Flow currently has no gate before external actions. An agent can post a PR comment, trigger a deploy, or merge — all without a human checkpoint. This adds that checkpoint as a first-class primitive.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Any action tagged as external/irreversible is staged, not immediately executed
- Staged action is surfaced to the user with full context (what, where, why)
- /approve executes it; /reject discards it with optional reason
- Orchestrator treats a staged action the same as a blocker — work pauses until resolved
- Works for GitHub actions, deploys, and any future external integrations

items.title: Execution history per item
items.description: Store what actually happened on each item — not just status changes, but agent actions, findings, decisions made, and why. Answers 'why does #7 have a reopen penalty?' and makes the scoring model explainable. Foundation for feedback-aware re-scoring.

links.reason: Result ingestion produces the raw data that execution history organizes

items.title: Graph view resets zoom/pan on background poll, same issue on the board when scroll down
items.description:
Every background data refresh re-renders the graph diagram and resets the viewport zoom/pan position to fit-to-screen. This makes it impossible to explore a specific area of the graph after zooming in. Should preserve viewport transform state across polls and only reset on explicit user action (e.g. switching away from graph view and back).

When viewing long lane, the UI refresh and the user moves back to the top of the page.

items.title: Work brief → agent handoff
items.description:
Turn the scored work brief into an actual agent invocation via `backlog handoff <agent>` CLI command. The brief already exists via /api/pulse — this makes it executable.

**Mechanism**: Shell out to `claude` CLI subprocess with a structured prompt assembled from the pulse response: task title, description, linked items, scoring rationale, constraints, and agent persona from .claude/agents/<name>.md.

**Structured prompt must include**:
- Item details (title, description, tags, complexity, priority)
- Linked items with relationship type and reason
- Tribunal justification (why this item was picked, lens scores)
- Agent persona (skills, learnings from .claude/agents/<name>.md)
- Output contract: agent must produce a JSON completion report on finish

**JSON completion report schema** (stdout, parseable by #3 result ingestion):
```json
{
  "item_id": "a1b2c3d4",
  "status": "done" | "blocked" | "partial",
  "summary": "...",
  "bugs_found": [{"title": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "follow_ups": [{"title": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "blocker": "..." // if status=blocked
}
```

**Implementation plan**:
1. New CLI command: `backlog handoff <agent> [--item <pos>] [--dry-run]`
2. If --item not specified, use pulse to pick the top recommended item for that agent
3. Assemble structured prompt from item data + pulse response + agent persona
4. Shell out: `claude --print '<prompt>'` (non-interactive, capture output)
5. Parse JSON report from stdout, write to `handoff_results/<item_id>_<timestamp>.json`
6. On --dry-run, print the assembled prompt without invoking claude

**Acceptance criteria**:
- `backlog handoff backend-dev` picks top ready item, invokes claude CLI, saves result JSON
- `backlog handoff backend-dev --dry-run` prints assembled prompt to stdout
- `backlog handoff backend-dev --item 2` forces item #2 regardless of pulse recommendation
- Result JSON is valid and matches schema above
- Errors (claude not found, item not ready, no ready items) give clear messages

links.reason: Handoff must exist before result ingestion can receive anything back

items.title: Agent result ingestion
items.description:
When an agent finishes a task, Flow reads its JSON completion report from `handoff_results/` and automatically drives the backlog forward. This is the return path of the agentic loop — without it, handoff is a one-way street.

**Trigger:** Called by the orchestrator immediately after handoff completes. Also works standalone: `backlog ingest <result_file>` for manual or testing use.

**Result schema (defined by handoff):**
```json
{
  "item_id": "a1b2c3d4",
  "status": "done" | "blocked" | "partial",
  "summary": "...",
  "bugs_found": [{"title": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "follow_ups": [{"title": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "blocker": "..."  // if status=blocked
}
```

**Status handling:**

`done` → advance item to next lane (code-review if it exists, otherwise inline review gate, then done). Orchestrator initiates reviewer agent.

`blocked` → item stays in-progress. Dev agent opens a thread on the item tagging the lead agent (`waiting_on: "lead"`) with the blocker description. Lead agent reads it, either resolves the blocker directly or escalates to human with context. Human only sees it if lead cannot resolve it.

`partial` → item stays in-progress. Dev agent opens a thread tagging lead agent with what was completed and what remains. Lead agent clarifies the gap, updates the item description if needed, and re-initiates the dev agent with full context. Human escalation only if lead cannot determine how to proceed.

**Discovered items (bugs_found + follow_ups):**
- Each entry becomes a new backlog item appended to the bottom
- `bugs_found` → category: bug, priority_weight: 8 (default, adjustable)
- `follow_ups` → category: feature, priority_weight: 5 (default)
- Both linked back to the originating item with `discovered-during` type and a reason
- Tribunal re-runs after ingestion so new items are immediately scored and visible in pulse

**Score update:**
After ingestion, re-compute scores for all items affected by the state change — the completed item, any newly created items, and items it was blocking (unblock cascade).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `backlog ingest handoff_results/a1b2c3d4_timestamp.json` processes the report correctly
- `done` → item advances to next lane, reviewer initiated
- `blocked` → thread opened on item, tagged lead, item stays in-progress
- `partial` → thread opened on item, tagged lead, item stays in-progress
- bugs_found and follow_ups become new backlog items with correct links and default scores
- Tribunal re-runs after ingestion
- Invalid or missing result file gives a clear error

links.reason: Handoff defines the result schema that ingestion parses
Orchestrator cannot close the loop without result ingestion processing agent outputs

items.title: Cold-start UX
items.description:
A new user clones Flow and has no backlog.json, no context, no idea what to say. This item covers the full day-1 experience across CLI and board.

**`backlog init` output:**
Currently prints nothing after creating the file. Should print a concise 'what's next' guide:
```
Created backlog.json

Next steps:
  backlog add "Your first task"          # add an item
  backlog board                           # open the visual board
  backlog list                            # view your backlog
```

**Board empty state:**
When `items` is empty, replace the blank columns with a welcome panel:
- Brief one-liner: what Flow is
- 3 copy-ready commands: `backlog add`, `backlog board`, `backlog orchestrate`
- A note pointing to README for full setup

Do NOT render fake example cards — that would be confusing when the board is shared with real agents.

**Board: no agents configured warning:**
If `config.agents` is empty and the board is opened, show a dismissible banner in the Settings panel:
> No agents configured — assignment intelligence is disabled. Add agents in Settings or edit backlog.json.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `backlog init` prints a next-steps guide after creating the file
- Empty board shows a welcome panel instead of empty columns
- Welcome panel has copy-ready commands, no fake data
- Settings panel surfaces 'no agents' warning when config.agents is empty
- All text is concise — onboarding should feel light, not overwhelming

items.title: Feedback-aware re-scoring
items.description: After each agent execution, capture an outcome signal (success, partial, reopened, blocked) and feed it back into the scoring model. Simple at first — thumbs up/down or inferred from reopen/done status — but the foundation matters. Over time, Flow gets smarter about what it recommends.

links.reason: Execution history provides the signal that re-scoring learns from

items.title: Lane history: track actor on every transition
items.description:
Currently lane_history entries are bare strings for early moves (e.g. 'backlog', 'refined', 'ready') with no record of who made the transition or when. Only agent-driven moves include a structured object with `lane`, `at`, and `by` fields.

Every lane transition — whether done by a human, the CLI, or an agent — should record the same structured format:
```json
{"lane": "refined", "at": "<iso-timestamp>", "by": "<actor>"}
```

Actor: agent name if known, `"user"` for CLI/board moves without an explicit actor.

**Implementation:**
- `apply_lane_transition` in core.py already writes structured format — no change needed there
- Fix the write path: any place that appends bare strings to `lane_history` directly (bypassing `apply_lane_transition`) must be updated
- Lazy normalization at read-time: when serializing items for the API or board, normalize bare strings to `{"lane": "<value>", "at": null, "by": "unknown"}` — no migration command needed
- `_extract_lane` already handles both formats for gate enforcement

**Acceptance criteria:**
- All new transitions write structured format regardless of caller path
- API responses normalize bare strings — board never sees raw strings
- Existing bare-string entries in backlog.json do not cause errors
- `who moved #3 to refined?` is answerable from lane_history

links.reason: Execution history per item depends on accurate actor-stamped lane transitions

items.title: Code review assignment — who reviews agent work?
items.description:
When an agent moves an item to code-review, there is no mechanism to assign a reviewer or notify anyone. The item sits in code-review indefinitely with no owner.

Needs a solution for:
1. **Reviewer assignment**: `assigned_to` currently tracks the worker — we need a separate `reviewer` field (or a convention like `assigned_to` transfers on code-review entry)
2. **Notification / visibility**: reviewer should be surfaced in pulse, board, and CLI show output
3. **Policy hook**: a policy rule like `on: enter_lane(code-review) → assign_reviewer(lead)` would make this automatic

Decisions needed before refining: does reviewer replace or supplement assigned_to? Is 'lead' a special agent role or just a config value?

items.title: Review gate fires repeatedly on long-running in-progress items
items.description:
The review gate check in `_orchestrate_tick` (cli.py) fires on every tick for any `in-progress` item where `not _review_gate_satisfied(item) and not _has_review_lane(statuses_list)`. If the inline review handoff fails silently, the next tick dispatches another one — creating duplicate handoffs and burning agent capacity.

**Fix:** Write a sentinel thread on the item when an inline review is dispatched, and check for it before dispatching again.

Sentinel thread schema:
```json
{
  "id": "inline-review-<item_id>",
  "topic": "inline-review-dispatched",
  "waiting_on": "agent",
  "body": "Inline review dispatched to <reviewer> at <timestamp>.",
  "resolved": false
}
```

Before dispatching:
1. Check if any thread with `id == f'inline-review-{item_id}'` and `resolved == False` exists
2. If yes → skip (review already in flight)
3. If no → dispatch handoff, write the sentinel thread

When the review result comes back and is ingested, the ingest path must resolve this thread (set `resolved: true`) so the gate knows the review cycle is complete.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Second tick does not dispatch a second inline review if the first is still in flight
- Sentinel thread is visible on the board (explains what happened)
- Sentinel thread is resolved when ingest processes the review result
- Works correctly when `require_review: false` — gate is skipped entirely, no sentinel written

links.reason: Found during review gate implementation — gate has no idempotency guard

items.title: Orchestrator observability
items.description:
When `backlog orchestrate` is running, there is no way to see what it's doing from the board. An item can sit in-progress for hours with no signal about whether the orchestrator is actively managing it or has silently stalled.

**Heartbeat file:**
The orchestrator writes `.orchestrator_state.json` (alongside `backlog.json`) atomically on each tick:
```json
{
  "running": true,
  "mode": "supervised",
  "last_tick": "<ISO UTC>",
  "items_in_flight": ["<item_id>", ...],
  "pending_result_files": 2
}
```
Written on every tick, not at startup. Absence of the file (or `last_tick` older than 2× poll interval) means the orchestrator is not running.

**API endpoint:**
`GET /api/orchestrator/status` — server reads `.orchestrator_state.json` and augments with stuck item detection:
```json
{
  "running": true,
  "mode": "supervised",
  "last_tick": "<ISO UTC>",
  "last_tick_age_seconds": 8,
  "items_in_flight": [...],
  "pending_result_files": 2,
  "stuck_items": [{"id": "...", "title": "...", "stuck_minutes": 45}]
}
```

**Stuck item detection (server-side):**
An item is stuck if: status is `in-progress`, `updated_at` is older than `config.orchestrator.stuck_threshold_minutes` (default 30), and no unresolved thread with `waiting_on: agent` exists (which would mean a handoff is legitimately in-flight).

**Board UI:**
- Header pill: `● Orchestrator running` (green) / `○ Orchestrator stopped` (grey), shows last tick age
- Card badge on stuck items: `⚠ stuck 45m`
- Polls `/api/orchestrator/status` on the same 5s interval as the board refresh

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Heartbeat file written atomically on every tick
- `GET /api/orchestrator/status` returns correct state when running and when stopped
- Board header shows running/stopped indicator with last tick age
- Stuck items get a visual badge after threshold
- `stuck_threshold_minutes` configurable, defaults to 30

links.reason: Orchestrator needs observability surface to be production-ready

items.title: GitHub Issues import bridge
items.description: One-way import from GitHub Issues into the backlog (`backlog import --from github-issues`). Dramatically lowers adoption barrier — users don't need a clean JSON backlog to start. Maps issue labels/milestones to Flow tags/complexity where possible. Read-only sync to start.

items.title: Show Item no in the UI when the item is opened
items.description: The Item no for the current item in the details screen is missing

items.title: Update SKILL.md, README.md, schema.md for staged actions API

Update README feature list and schema tree.

links.reason: Docs update after staged actions feature lands

links.reason: Test coverage after cold-start UX feature lands

items.description: After #14 merges: document GET /api/orchestrator/status endpoint, .orchestrator_state.json schema, config.orchestrator.stuck_threshold_minutes config field. Update README with observability feature section.

items.title: Add CLI tests for orchestrator observability
items.description: After #14 merges: add test_cli.py tests for heartbeat file writing (atomic, correct schema), stuck item detection logic, orchestrator status API. Optionally add evals.json NL scenario for 'is the orchestrator running'.

items.title: Agent work lands on feature branches with PR — reviewer merges to main
items.description:
Redesign the agent workflow so work is preserved on named branches and reviewed against real diffs.

**Execution model:**
- Work Brief includes explicit git instructions: create `feat/item-{id}-{slug}` branch, commit before finishing
- Real execution: Claude-as-orchestrator → Agent tool (worktree isolation) → sub-agent has full shell/file access
- Python `backlog orchestrate` CLI = board monitor + prompt assembler only (not agent launcher)
- `claude --print` / Python handoff path = dry-run/debug only

**Work agent responsibilities:**
1. `git checkout -b feat/item-{id}-{slug}` at start
2. Implement, commit with clear messages
3. Move item to `code-review`, output result JSON including `branch_name`
4. Do NOT push — commits stay local until human batch-pushes

**Review:**
- Reviewer checks out the branch (not diff text) — reads full implementation as a senior engineer
- Socratic methodology (#37 doctrine): ask questions before running checklist
- Reviewer must give precise blockers: file:line:what. Architectural feedback = escalate to user, not reject
- Review Brief includes: branch name, spec, acceptance criteria

**Merge / rejection:**
- Pass → orchestrator runs `git merge --no-ff feat/item-{id}-{slug}` → marks done
- Reject (Case 1 — surgical) → orchestrator re-invokes work agent with branch + git log + exact blocker list
  - For one-line fixes: orchestrator may address directly without re-invoking agent
- Reject (Case 2 — architectural) → escalate to user (this is a refinement failure, not a review finding)
- Merge conflicts → escalate to user

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Work Brief template includes branch creation + commit instructions
- Work result JSON includes `branch_name` field
- Review Brief includes branch name; reviewer checks it out and reads full code
- Orchestrator merges branch after pass verdict
- Orchestrator handles surgical reject loop (re-invoke or self-fix)
- Architectural rejections escalate to user, not to work agent
- No agent pushes to remote — human controls push
- Works with parallel items (each gets its own branch)

links.reason: #37 defines the Socratic review methodology the reviewer uses on the branch surface created by #22

threads.topic: Execution model: claude --print vs real tool-use session
threads.body:
Current `backlog handoff` uses `claude --print <prompt>` — a stateless text-generation call with no tool access. Agents can't create git branches or commit in this mode.

Two paths forward:
(a) Keep `--print` but enhance the Work Brief to include explicit git instructions (`git checkout -b feat/item-{id}-{slug}`, commit, push) — these only execute when the agent is a real Claude Code session with shell access (e.g. via Agent SDK worktree). The `--print` path becomes a dry-run / planning path.

(b) Change `backlog handoff` to invoke a full interactive `claude` session with file/shell tool access, not `--print`. The JSON output contract stays, but the agent executes git ops inline.

Which path is intended?
threads.resolution: Decision (a): Work Brief includes explicit git instructions (checkout branch, commit, push). Real execution path: Claude-as-orchestrator → Agent tool (worktree isolation) → sub-agent executes git ops. Python `backlog orchestrate` CLI is board monitor + prompt assembler only; not the agent launcher. `claude --print` path is dry-run/debug only.

threads.topic: What surface does the reviewer actually review?
threads.body:
Two options:
(a) Review Brief pipes `git diff feat/item-X...main` text directly into the prompt — reviewer reads the diff as text, no branch access needed.

(b) Review Brief tells the reviewer the branch name; reviewer checks out / diffs it as part of executing the review.

Option (a) is simpler and works in any invocation mode. Option (b) is richer (can read full file context) but requires real tool-use session. Which is preferred?
threads.resolution: Decision (b): Reviewer checks out the branch and reads full code — not just diff text. Review Brief includes branch name. Reviewer navigates implementation as a senior engineer would: follows call chains, reads context, asks Socratic questions (#37 doctrine applies here). #22 and #37 are companion items — #22 is the surface, #37 is the methodology.

threads.body:
Three options:
(a) Reviewer merges as part of issuing `pass` verdict.
(b) Orchestrator merges in `ingest` after processing a `pass` result — cleaner separation of concerns, orchestrator owns git state.
(c) Human approves merge after reviewer passes — Flow opens the PR but human clicks merge.

Given the autonomous-flow philosophy, (b) seems most consistent. Confirm?
threads.resolution: Decision: Orchestrator merges mechanically after ingesting a pass verdict (reads branch_name from work result JSON, runs git merge --no-ff, marks done). Reviewer does not merge. On reject: only Case 1 (tactical/surgical) rejections are valid — reviewer must give file:line:what. For one-line fixes the orchestrator may address directly without re-invoking the work agent. Architectural rejections are a refinement failure and escalate to user, not the work agent. Human controls push to remote — agents commit locally only. Responsibility rule: orchestrator is accountable for spec quality before handoff, including for casual user requests — no vague brief reaches a work agent.

threads.body:
**Blocker 1** — cli.py:1310-1334: Surgical reject re-invoke is not idempotent. After ingest moves item back to in-progress, next tick's step-4 work-dispatch loop has no guard for an unresolved waiting_on:agent thread — will double-dispatch work agents on every tick until a result file arrives. Fix: before dispatching work in step-4, check for an unresolved thread with waiting_on='agent'; if present, skip.

**Blocker 2** — cli.py:1290, cli.py:1330, cli.py:1358: All three store.write(data2) calls after ingest omit expected_version. ingest_result already incremented the version; these writes replace the file with a stale snapshot, bypassing optimistic locking. Fix: re-read backlog after ingest_result returns, then write with current version.
threads.resolution: Fixed: (1) added unresolved waiting_on=agent thread guard in step-4 dispatch loop; (2) all three store.write(data2) calls now pass expected_version

threads.body:
**Blocker** — core.py:431-439: surgical-reject thread uses random _generate_id() ID; ingest_result only resolved the inline-review-{item_id} sentinel, leaving the random-ID thread unresolved. After re-work lands and item advances to code-review, step-4 guard sees the unresolved waiting_on=agent thread and skips forever — reviewer never dispatched.

Fix: ingest_result now resolves ALL unresolved waiting_on=agent threads when a result lands, not just the named sentinel.
threads.resolution: Fixed: ingest_result now resolves all unresolved waiting_on=agent threads, not just the named sentinel

threads.topic: Follow-up: pre-existing store.write() calls missing expected_version (cli.py:1383, 1502, 1519)
threads.body: Three pre-existing store.write(data) calls in the orchestrator tick lack expected_version (lines 1383, 1502, 1519). Not regressions from #22 — low collision probability — but inconsistent with all other write paths. Worth a cleanup pass.

threads.topic: Follow-up: Review Brief silently falls back to computed slug when metadata.branch_name absent (cli.py:597)
threads.body: Review Brief uses computed slug as branch name fallback when item.metadata.branch_name is not set. Could mislead reviewer if actual branch diverged. Consider failing loudly or omitting the branch section rather than fabricating a name.

items.title: Bake Superpowers methodology into agent personas
items.description: Extract key practices from obra/superpowers skills and embed them into .claude/agents/*.md persona files. Three skills to incorporate: (1) test-driven-development — RED-GREEN-REFACTOR enforcement, no production code without failing test first, delete code written before tests. Add to backend-dev and frontend-dev. (2) systematic-debugging — 4-phase root cause process (investigate → pattern analysis → hypothesis → fix), no fixes without root cause, stop after 3 failed fixes and question architecture. Add to all agents. (3) verification-before-completion — evidence before claims, run verification commands before any completion claim, never trust agent self-reports. Add to all agents and strengthen reviewer's checklist. Do NOT install superpowers as a plugin — it conflicts with Flow's orchestration layer. Cherry-pick methodology only.

items.title: backlog doctor CLI command — validate install health
items.description: Add a 'backlog doctor' command that checks: (1) assets directory exists and contains backlog-board.html, (2) all timestamps in backlog.json parse without naive/aware conflicts, (3) backlog.json schema is valid (required fields, valid statuses, link integrity). Outputs a pass/fail checklist so users can self-diagnose install issues instead of debugging manually.

items.title: Enrich Flow's internal skills with engineering doctrine
items.description: Flow's .claude/skills/ (review-changes, debug-issue, refactor-safely, explore-codebase) tell the agent which tools to run but lack engineering doctrine — what to look for, what 'good enough' means, approval criteria, anti-patterns. Gap identified vs addyosmani/agent-skills: our skills are tool-invocation scripts; his are senior-engineer playbooks. Missing coverage: spec-driven-development, TDD, security-and-hardening, performance-optimization, shipping-and-launch, deprecation-and-migration, documentation-and-ADRs, CI/CD. Enrich existing skills with checklists and criteria, and add the missing SDLC phase skills.

items.title: Add spec-driven-development skill
items.description: Flow's orchestrator starts coding with no spec discipline — agents guess requirements. Add a .claude/skills/spec-driven-development.md that enforces spec-before-code: PRD structure, acceptance criteria format, unknowns surfacing, and how to block the agent from coding until spec is approved. Should integrate with backlog refinement phase.

items.title: Add shipping-and-launch skill
items.description: Flow has a review gate but no rollout discipline. Add a .claude/skills/shipping-and-launch.md covering staged deploys, feature flags, rollback criteria, launch checklists, and what 'done' means in production vs done in code-review.

items.title: Enrich review-changes skill with five-axis doctrine
items.description: review-changes.md currently only lists which graph tools to run. Enrich it with: five review axes (correctness, readability, architecture, security, performance), approval standard (what 'good enough' means), anti-patterns checklist, and explicit merge/reject criteria. Based on addyosmani/agent-skills code-review-and-quality.

items.title: Enrich debug-issue skill with triage doctrine
items.description: debug-issue.md only lists graph tool invocations. Enrich with: stop-the-line rule, structured triage checklist (reproduce → isolate → fix → guard), reproducibility decision trees, and non-reproducible bug handling. Based on addyosmani/agent-skills debugging-and-error-recovery.

items.title: Enrich backlog-manager decomposition with task-sizing doctrine
items.description: Backlog decomposition guidance is thin on what makes a good atomic task. Enrich SKILL.md decomposition section with: task sizing rules (one agent, one session), dependency graph mapping, explicit parallel vs sequential identification, acceptance criteria format, and how to spot over-decomposition. Based on addyosmani/agent-skills planning-and-task-breakdown.

items.title: Add test-driven-development skill
items.description: No TDD forcing function exists in Flow. Add a .claude/skills/test-driven-development.md with red/green/refactor discipline, test-first contract, what counts as a passing test vs a passing suite, and how agents should gate implementation on failing tests first.

items.title: Add security-and-hardening skill
items.description: Zero security gates exist anywhere in Flow's pipeline. Add a .claude/skills/security-and-hardening.md covering threat modeling, OWASP top 10 checks, input validation, secret handling, and a security review checklist agents must run before moving to code-review lane.

items.title: Enrich refactor-safely and explore-codebase skills with doctrine
items.description: refactor-safely.md and explore-codebase.md are thin tool-invocation scripts like review-changes was. Enrich both: refactor-safely needs safety criteria, when-not-to-refactor rules, and scope creep guards; explore-codebase needs guidance on what to look for when navigating unfamiliar code.

items.title: Add source-driven-development skill
items.description: Flow agents sometimes hallucinate APIs or guess at interfaces. Add a .claude/skills/source-driven-development.md enforcing: read the source before coding, never assume function signatures, verify imports exist, check actual return types. Directly addresses a known failure mode in agent-written code.

items.title: Spec gate before ready — enforce written spec on refined → ready transition
items.description: Before an item can move from refined → ready, require a written spec covering: (1) acceptance criteria — what does done look like exactly, (2) failure modes — what happens when it goes wrong, (3) edge cases — empty inputs, missing files, timeouts, concurrent writes. The spec answers are embedded into the item description and the spec_written readiness signal (+10%) is set automatically. This gives the existing signal real weight instead of being opt-in noise. The skill's Refine operation should drive this: when the user resolves the last thread and says 'mark ready', if no spec exists yet, surface the three questions above before allowing the transition. Goal: no item enters the execution queue without someone having written down what done actually looks like.

items.title: Behavioral contracts in refinement — capture failure modes and semantics before work starts
items.description: During the Refine operation, surface structured questions that capture semantic context beyond a plain description: (1) failure modes — what does failure look like and how should it be handled? silent fail? throw? retry? (2) performance expectations — any latency, throughput, or size constraints? (3) retry semantics — is this operation idempotent? safe to retry on failure? (4) behavioral contracts — what invariants must hold before and after? These answers become part of the item description as a 'Contracts' block before work starts. This means the implementing agent receives not just 'what to build' but 'how it must behave under stress'. Links to #35 (spec gate) — contracts are part of the spec. Without this, agents implement the happy path and leave failure behavior undefined, which surfaces as bugs in code review.

items.title: Socratic code review — generate senior-engineer questions before running checklist
items.description:
Upgrade the Code Review Protocol in SKILL.md: before running the static checklist (correctness, idempotency, error handling, edge cases, write safety), first generate the questions a skeptical senior engineer would ask about this specific implementation. The questions are derived from the item's acceptance criteria, behavioral contracts, and the actual code — not from a generic template. The reviewer then answers each question in the review artifact. This makes review item-context-aware rather than checklist-mechanical. Example: for a concurrent write operation the generated questions might be 'What happens if two agents pick this item simultaneously?' and 'Is the version check atomic with the write?' — questions the static checklist would not surface. The static checklist remains as a floor, but the Socratic questions are the ceiling. This is particularly valuable for catching assumptions the implementer made that were never written down in the spec.

## Spec
**Acceptance criteria:** Socratic questions are generated from the item's acceptance criteria, behavioral contracts, and the actual code. The reviewer answers each question inline as part of the review artifact — questions serve as a lens, not a pre-read gate. Static checklist remains but Socratic questions are the ceiling.
**Failure modes:** Items should always have spec/acceptance criteria from the refinement/lead agent. If missing (e.g. user created and moved to ready manually), the reviewer asks the user for acceptance criteria before proceeding rather than skipping the Socratic step silently.
**Edge cases:** Add Socratic questions step to the existing Code Review Protocol section in SKILL.md — not a new section. Keeps all review guidance in one place.

links.reason: #22 creates the branch-based review surface that #37's Socratic methodology operates on

items.title: Flywheel closure on complete — capture learnings that feed back into future specs
items.description: When marking an item done, the Complete operation should require one closing step before finishing: the completing agent answers two questions — (1) 'What was surprising about this implementation?' and (2) 'What would you spec differently if you started again?' The answer goes directly into the agent's Learnings in their persona file (.claude/agents/<name>.md). This closes the loop: spec → work → review → learning → better specs next time. Currently the loop is broken — persona Learnings exist but nothing forces agents to write back from completed work. Without explicit closure, every cycle starts fresh. The flywheel only spins if this step is mandatory, not optional. Note: if the answer is 'nothing was surprising', that is a valid answer and takes 5 seconds — the prompt cost is low, the compounding value is high.

items.title: Update SKILL.md, README.md, schema.md for branch workflow (#22)
items.description:
Document the agent branch workflow introduced in #22:

- Work Brief now includes `## Git Instructions` (branch create + commit)
- Work result output contract has a new `branch_name` field
- Review Brief now includes `## Branch` section with checkout instruction
- Orchestrator merges branch on passing review (`git merge --no-ff`)
- Surgical vs architectural reject handling (file:line detection → re-invoke vs user escalation)

Update all three docs to reflect these contracts and behaviors.

links.reason: Documents the branch workflow, output contract, and orchestrator merge behavior introduced in #22

items.title: Enrich SKILL.md — agents must add follow-up backlog items proactively
items.description:
Two changes:

**1. SKILL.md — agent-initiated follow-ups**
Add explicit instruction to the 'Agent-initiated items' section:
Agents must add follow-up backlog items immediately when they discover:
- Doc gaps created by the work just done (SKILL.md, README.md, schema.md)
- Pre-existing bugs or tech debt surfaced during implementation or review
- Warnings from code review that don't block merge but need future attention
- Any natural consequence of the completed work that isn't yet tracked

Rule: don't ask the user — add it, link it to the source item, and mention it in the completion summary.

**2. SKILL.md + policy — doctrine-before-feature ordering**
The tribunal scores by priority_weight and dependency links. It cannot detect that a low-complexity doctrine/doc item enables better execution of a higher-scored feature — because there is no structural `blocks` link and no shared tag signal.

Two additions to close this:
- Add a SKILL.md heuristic: when a low-complexity `docs` or `skill` item directly enables an in-flight or next-up feature (same tag area, linked), it should be treated as if it blocks that feature for prioritization purposes — add a `blocks` link explicitly at creation time.
- Add a structured policy: low-complexity `docs`/`skill` items tagged with active feature tags and linked to a `backlog`/`ready` item with pw >= 8 get elevated to pw+2. This makes the tribunal surface them correctly without human intervention.

links.reason: #39 was the immediate fix; this makes it doctrine so all agents follow the same rule
#40 doctrine teaches agents to handle follow-ups and ordering — #23 agent spawn benefits from this before it runs

items.title: Wire backlog top to use tribunal ranking instead of score-only
items.description:
Replace compute_scores in backlog top with evaluate_tribunal. Pull ranked list from shadow_ranking, show tribunal score + justification for top pick. Also investigate why justification returns None. ~15-20 lines in cli.py.

## Spec
**Acceptance criteria:** backlog top always uses tribunal ranking (6 lenses: urgency, leverage, strategic fit, risk, momentum, agent_fit). Raw score is no longer the sort order. Tribunal wins on any ordering difference.
**Failure modes:** Empty backlog → print 'Backlog is empty.' and exit 0. Any tribunal error → show error to user, do not silently fall back to raw score.
**Edge cases:** If justification is None or missing, display 'No justification available' — never omit it silently as users will ask why.

items.title: backlog doctor should detect when installed SKILL.md is out of sync with repo
